text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC
ROUTINE SURVEILLANCE VISIT

PATIENT: Margaret Delacroix
ATTENDING: Gynecologic Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine surveillance follow-up for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, currently on niraparib maintenance therapy. Patient presents for scheduled 3-month visit. No new complaints.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Delacroix is a 54-year-old postmenopausal woman of French-Canadian descent with a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC, also referred to as c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74 - an Eastern European founder mutation) and a history of FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (pT3c pN1 M0, AJCC 8th edition), originally diagnosed approximately 27 months ago. She presents today for her routine surveillance visit while on niraparib maintenance therapy, now at 18 months of treatment.

To briefly recap her history: she initially presented with a 3-month history of abdominal distension, early satiety, and unintentional weight loss, ultimately found to have bilateral ovarian masses with extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis, omental caking, and pelvic/para-aortic nodal involvement. CA-125 at diagnosis was 1,847 U/mL. Molecular profiling confirmed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant with somatic LOH at the BRCA1 locus (biallelic inactivation), TP53 p.R248W, CCNE1 amplification, and an HRD score of 56 (HRD-positive). She underwent primary debulking surgery approximately 27 months ago achieving R0 resection (complete gross cytoreduction), followed by 6 cycles of carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 (paclitaxel dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 for cycles 5 and 6 due to Grade 3 peripheral neuropathy). CA-125 normalized to 18 U/mL at end of treatment; post-treatment PET-CT showed complete metabolic response with no FDG-avid disease.

She was initiated on niraparib maintenance at an individualized starting dose of 200 mg orally once daily approximately 18 months ago. At week 3 of niraparib, she developed Grade 3 thrombocytopenia (platelets nadir 42,000/uL), requiring a hold and subsequent dose reduction to 150 mg daily. Since the dose reduction, her platelet counts have been stable in the Grade 1 range (105,000-135,000/uL at prior visits) and she has not required further dose adjustments. She has tolerated niraparib 150 mg daily well overall.

Today, she reports feeling well. She has returned to full-time work as a high school librarian and is managing her daily activities without significant limitations. She denies any new abdominal or pelvic symptoms - specifically no abdominal pain, no bloating, no early satiety, no change in bowel habits, and no new pelvic pressure. She denies any new back pain, dyspnea, or chest discomfort. She takes niraparib 150 mg every evening with food and reports good adherence - she uses a pill organizer and has not missed doses. She continues venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily for menopausal vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes substantially improved). She uses vaginal lubricants for vaginal dryness. She is not on hormone replacement therapy given cancer history.

Regarding her prior neuropathy: the Grade 3 peripheral neuropathy and left foot drop she developed during chemotherapy have largely resolved. She reports only very mild residual tingling in the tips of both big toes with prolonged standing; she finds this essentially negligible in her day-to-day life and does not consider it bothersome. She completed physical therapy for foot drop, which resolved fully. Fine motor function is intact.

Psychosocially, she has been connected with the ovarian cancer survivor support group through oncology social work. She acknowledges ongoing mild background anxiety related to recurrence fear, which she describes as manageable and not debilitating. She has found the support group helpful. Her daughter (age 26) confirmed BRCA1 carrier status at testing approximately 12 months ago and is now engaged with a high-risk surveillance program.

Most recent surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis (performed approximately 6 months prior) showed no evidence of disease. CA-125 at that time was 12 U/mL. Most recent breast MRI (performed approximately 2 months prior) was negative.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: No unintentional weight loss, no fevers, no night sweats. Mild intermittent fatigue (Grade 1, not limiting activities).
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, no vomiting, no abdominal pain, no early satiety, no change in bowel habits, no rectal bleeding. Occasional mild constipation managed with dietary fiber.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, no dysuria. Vaginal dryness present and managed non-hormonally.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain, no joint pain. Very mild residual toe paresthesias bilaterally as noted above.
Neurological: No new neurological complaints. Residual minimal toe tingling as described.
Respiratory: No dyspnea, no cough, no hemoptysis.
Hematologic: No unusual bruising, no petechiae, no gum bleeding, no epistaxis. No symptoms of anemia (no exertional dyspnea or palpitations beyond baseline).
Psychiatric: Mild recurrence-related anxiety, well-managed with support group participation.
Endocrine/Reproductive: Menopausal symptoms stable and well-managed.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Appears stated age. Hair regrowth complete and full.
Vital signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, Heart rate 72 bpm (regular), Temperature 98.4 F, Respiratory rate 14, SpO2 98% on room air.
Weight: 73 kg. BMI: 26.7 kg/m2.
Performance status: ECOG 0.
HEENT: Atraumatic, normocephalic. Sclerae anicteric. No lymphadenopathy in cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary chains.
Breast: No dominant masses bilaterally. No skin changes. No axillary lymphadenopathy. (Bilateral breast MRI reviewed - negative, performed 2 months ago.)
Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No pleural dullness.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable masses. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. Midline laparotomy scar well-healed. No shifting dullness or fluid wave.
Pelvic (external): Normal post-operative appearance. Vaginal vault smooth and non-tender on exam. No palpable pelvic masses. No cuff nodularity.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No petechiae or ecchymoses. Good capillary refill.
Neurological: Cranial nerves intact. Strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Gait normal, no foot drop. Sensation: intact to light touch in bilateral lower extremities; very mild decreased sensation to fine touch at bilateral great toe tips - stable from prior exam. Romberg negative.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC (today):
  - WBC: 5.8 x10^9/L (normal)
  - ANC: 2,100/uL (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 11.1 g/dL (mildly low, Grade 1, stable from prior)
  - Platelets: 119,000/uL (mildly low, Grade 1 thrombocytopenia; stable in this range since dose reduction to 150 mg)
Comprehensive metabolic panel:
  - Creatinine: 0.80 mg/dL (stable, no renal toxicity from prior platinum)
  - BUN: 14 mg/dL
  - LFTs: AST 22, ALT 19, alkaline phosphatase 68, total bilirubin 0.7 (all within normal limits)
  - Electrolytes: within normal limits
CA-125: 11 U/mL (normal; stable/slightly lower than 12 U/mL at last visit - biochemical complete remission maintained)
No peripheral blood differential abnormalities to suggest dysplastic features; morphology on prior CBC differentials has been unremarkable throughout niraparib treatment.

---

IMAGING
Most recent CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (approximately 6 months ago): No evidence of disease. No ascites, no peritoneal implants, no lymphadenopathy, no suspicious lesions. Post-surgical pelvic changes stable.
Most recent breast MRI (approximately 2 months ago): No suspicious enhancement. No masses. BI-RADS 1. Negative.
Next scheduled surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis due in approximately 6 months.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, FIGO STAGE IIIC, pT3c pN1 M0 - COMPLETE REMISSION
Ms. Delacroix remains in excellent and durable complete remission at 27 months from her original diagnosis. CA-125 is 11 U/mL, stable and well within normal limits. She is clinically, biochemically, and radiographically NED. Her disease course has been favorable: R0 cytoreduction followed by complete normalization of CA-125 with platinum-taxane chemotherapy, and now 18 months of uninterrupted niraparib maintenance without any evidence of disease recurrence. This is an encouraging trajectory for a BRCA1-mutant, HRD-positive, completely debulked FIGO IIIC patient. Will continue niraparib maintenance without modification at this time. Planned treatment duration: indefinite, until progression or unacceptable toxicity, consistent with SOLO-1 and PRIMA trial maintenance frameworks.

2. NIRAPARIB MAINTENANCE THERAPY - 150 mg ORALLY ONCE DAILY
Patient has been on niraparib 150 mg daily (dose-reduced from 200 mg ISD due to Grade 3 thrombocytopenia early in treatment) for 18 months. Current tolerability is good at this dose level. CBC stable: platelets 119,000/uL (Grade 1, asymptomatic), hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, stable, no transfusion required, no erythropoiesis-stimulating agent initiated). ANC normal. No evidence of clinically meaningful myelosuppression beyond the stable Grade 1 cytopenias. No morphologic concern for MDS on CBC differential review (no dysplastic changes, no blast forms on smear). Routine CBC and chemistry monitoring will continue monthly. Given prolonged therapy (approaching 2 years), will formally discuss risk of secondary malignancy (MDS/AML, a known rare but serious complication of long-term PARP inhibitor use) at next visit; incidence is low but patients on extended maintenance should be counseled annually. No changes to niraparib dosing today.

3. RESIDUAL PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY - GRADE 1, STABLE
Minimal residual bilateral toe paresthesias, essentially stable and non-progressive. No functional limitation. Physical therapy for prior foot drop concluded successfully. Continued observation. If any worsening noted, neurology follow-up available.

4. BRCA1 GERMLINE PATHOGENIC VARIANT (5382insC) - ONGOING SURVEILLANCE AND FAMILY IMPLICATIONS
Patient is post-bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (accomplished at cytoreductive surgery). Primary ovarian/tubal cancer risk already manifested and addressed surgically. Breast cancer risk remains elevated given BRCA1 carrier status. Breast surveillance: annual mammography and annual breast MRI, both current and negative. Option for risk-reducing mastectomy has been discussed on multiple prior visits; patient continues to prefer active surveillance over prophylactic surgery, which is a reasonable and guideline-supported approach. Will continue annual breast MRI and mammography. Daughter confirmed BRCA1-positive; engaged with high-risk program - no further action needed from our end.

5. MENOPAUSAL SYMPTOMS
Surgical menopause well-managed with non-hormonal approaches. Venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily for vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes well-controlled). Vaginal lubricants for dryness. No systemic HRT given ovarian cancer diagnosis. Bone health: will ensure DEXA scan has been performed given surgical menopause - will place referral if not already completed; patient should be on calcium and vitamin D supplementation.

6. PSYCHOSOCIAL
Mild recurrence anxiety, well-managed with oncology social work engagement and ovarian cancer survivor support group participation. ECOG 0, returned to full-time work. No formal psychiatric intervention needed at this time; continue support group.

FOLLOW-UP: Return to clinic in 3 months for routine surveillance. Repeat CA-125 and CBC at that time. Next surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis in approximately 6 months. Breast MRI annually (next due in approximately 10 months). DEXA scan referral placed today if not already on file.

Time spent: 35 minutes, greater than 50% in counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed, Gynecologic Oncology Attending.
